The Ellison Medical Foundation awards the Senior Scholar Award to Dr. Roger Brent of the Molecular Sciences Institute

[BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA ­ November 27, 2000] The Ellison Medical Foundation has awarded Dr. Roger Brent, Associate Director of the Molecular Sciences Institute, the Senior Scholar Award for his substantial prior scientific creativity and productivity and for his current interest in developing a new research program in aging. The Ellison Senior Scholar Award includes funding which totals $850,000 over four years to support novel, high-risk research relevant to understanding aging processes. Brent's proposal, "Identification of protein regulators of self-renewal, differentiation, and senescence in embryonic stem cells" was one of only thirteen awards chosen this year by the foundation.

The Ellison Medical Foundation is a non-profit private foundation established by a gift from Larry Ellison, CEO of Oracle, to support basic biomedical research on aging relevant to understanding aging processes and age related diseases and disabilities. According to the foundation, the Senior Scholar Award "is intended to provide significant support to established investigators in order to allow development of new, creative research programs by investigators who may not currently be conducting aging research or who may wish to develop new research programs in aging."

With this support, Brent plans to use functional genomic tools to find regulatory proteins that control the perpetuation, differentiation, and senescence of embryonic stem cells. These functional genomic tools include the two-hybrid system and peptide aptamers. The two-hybrid system is an assay used by thousands of research labs around the world to detect protein-protein interactions in vitro. Brent and his co-workers identified the modular basis of eukaryotic transcription factors in the mid-80's, which served as the basis for the development of the two-hybrid system. Peptide aptamers are small proteins consisting of short stretches of random amino acids displayed on the surface of a larger protein. These aptamers can be used to perturb interactions of proteins by modulating their interactions or by disrupting them altogether. Brent's Lab has been instrumental in developing and using these research technologies to enhance understandings of proteins involved in the cell division cycle. As part of the Ellison Senior Scholar Award, Brent will use an enhanced version of the two-hybrid system and peptide aptamers to help identify proteins involved in the process of stem cell proliferation and differentiation.

Stem cells are self-replicating cells that divide to produce one daughter cell that differentiates into the many specialized types of cells that make up the tissues of the body and one daughter that remains a stem cell, thereby continuing to serve as a source of precursor differentiated cells. Stem cells therefore have the ability to express and regulate all molecules needed to develop into any of the 1000 or so distinct types of human cells. It is also a characteristic of stem cells to differentiate into specific cell types based on cues they receive from their surroundings. These qualities make stem cells an ideal candidate to study the possibility of regenerating damaged or aged tissues and organs.

Brent will collaborate with Dr. James Thomson of the University of Wisconsin on this work. Thomson's Lab was one of the first to isolate and work on embryonic stems cells from primate and human embryos. In this collaboration, Brent will provide technological expertise and the aptamers; Thomason will provide the cell culture expertise and the stem cells. As Thomson manipulates stems cells to differentiate into the numerous distinct types of human cells, Brent will use peptide aptamers to pull out and identify proteins involved in regulating each differentiation process. If novel proteins can be pulled out and identified in each process, they may eventually be used as targets or used to isolate targets for small-molecule drug discovery efforts.

The Ellison Medical Foundation's award extends from January 15, 2001 until January 14, 2005.
